critic_raw:
The most significant methodological problem is the use of stratified random split on clinical radiology reports from a single hospital over two years. Radiology reports contain temporal dependencies at multiple levels: radiologist practice drift, evolving diagnostic coding guidelines (ICD code versioning and updates), changes in referral patterns, and departmental protocol changes. A random split allows future reports — potentially coded under updated guidelines — to appear in training while earlier reports coded under older guidelines appear in the test set, or vice versa, producing a distribution mismatch that a temporal split would surface but a random split hides. The design acknowledges departmental variation but not temporal protocol drift, which is a more serious confounder.

The stratified random split for multi-label data is also technically underspecified. With multi-label outputs (multiple disease codes per report), stratification cannot perfectly balance all label combinations simultaneously — the standard stratified split approaches work for single labels. With a long-tail distribution of disease codes (common codes vs. rare codes), simple stratification on the most common labels will leave rare label combinations poorly balanced, and performance on rare labels is precisely where transformer models are expected to show gains over rule-based systems.

The rule-based baseline tuning is described as 'equivalent effort for fairness,' but keyword-based rule systems have fundamentally different tuning surfaces than transformer fine-tuning. A rule-based system's performance scales with the quality of the keyword vocabulary, whereas a transformer's performance scales with training data quantity and fine-tuning procedure. If both are tuned 'with equivalent effort' in wall-clock time or engineering time, the comparison will systematically favor whichever approach benefits more from the given budget — not a fair architectural comparison.

The design describes using Macro-F1 as the primary metric, which is appropriate for label imbalance. However, Macro-F1 averages performance equally across all disease codes, including extremely rare codes with very few test instances. With 10,000 reports and potentially hundreds of disease codes, some codes may have fewer than 10 test instances — making Macro-F1 dominated by statistical noise on rare labels. The design does not acknowledge this instability or propose confidence intervals or bootstrapped estimates of metric uncertainty.

Finally, the transformer model is fine-tuned on annotated radiology reports, but the annotation process is not clearly independent of the reports themselves. If annotations were derived semi-automatically from the text of the reports (a common clinical NLP pipeline), then fine-tuning on the reports with those annotations risks fitting to annotation artifacts rather than clinical content, and the comparison with a keyword-based system that uses the same underlying text becomes circularly self-referential.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores temporal protocol drift in clinical coding: ICD code versioning, changing radiology guidelines, and evolving departmental practices create temporal non-stationarity that a random split conceals, potentially mixing data from different coding regimes across train and test.

Multi-label stratified splitting is technically underspecified — standard stratification methods work for single labels; with long-tail multi-label distributions, rare label combinations will remain imbalanced, and performance on rare codes is precisely where the transformer is expected to gain.

The 'equivalent effort' tuning comparison between a keyword rule system and a transformer fine-tuning procedure conflates incomparable tuning surfaces — one scales with vocabulary quality, the other with training data and learning rate schedule — making fairness claims unverifiable.

Macro-F1 averaged over potentially hundreds of disease codes including rare codes with fewer than 10 test instances will be dominated by statistical noise; no confidence intervals or bootstrap estimates are proposed to bound metric uncertainty.

If disease code annotations were derived semi-automatically from report text, fine-tuning on the same text risks fitting annotation artifacts rather than clinical content, making the comparison with a keyword system that also reads the same text potentially circular.
